title: GenAI & LLM Fundamentals
description: Transformers, attention, tokenization, embeddings, decoding, context windows.

cards.front: Explain the transformer architecture at a high level.
cards.back: A neural network built around self-attention (no recurrence) that processes all tokens in parallel through a stack of identical blocks — each with multi-head self-attention plus a feed-forward MLP, wrapped in residual connections and layer norm.
distractors: A recurrent network that processes tokens one at a time, using LSTM gates to carry hidden state across the sequence.
A convolutional network that slides fixed-width filters over token embeddings to capture local n-gram patterns.
A graph neural network that passes messages between tokens along a parsed syntax tree.
A bag-of-words model that averages word embeddings and feeds them to a softmax classifier.
A diffusion model that iteratively denoises token embeddings over many steps to produce text.
An encoder-decoder of stacked GRUs joined by an attention bridge, usable only for machine translation.
cards.quizAnswer: A self-attention network (no recurrence) that processes all tokens in parallel through stacked attention + feed-forward blocks.

cards.back: Each token is projected into Query, Key and Value vectors; attention weights = softmax(Q·Kᵀ / √d_k), and the output is the weighted sum of the Value vectors — so each token's new representation blends all tokens' values by relevance.
distractors: Each token's embedding is multiplied by a fixed positional matrix and the results are summed to mix context.
A gating network decides which previous hidden state to carry forward, like an LSTM forget gate.
Queries and Keys are compared with cosine similarity and only the single highest-scoring Value is copied to the output.
Attention averages all token embeddings equally, then subtracts the mean to center the sequence.
Q, K and V are the same vector, so attention is just each token's dot product with itself scaled by √d_k.
Value vectors are convolved with a learned kernel followed by max-pooling over the sequence.
cards.quizAnswer: Project each token to Query/Key/Value; weights are softmax(Q·Kᵀ/√d_k), and the output is the weighted sum of Values.

cards.front: Why is positional encoding needed, and what kinds exist?
cards.back: Self-attention is permutation-invariant, so order must be injected. Kinds: sinusoidal (fixed), learned absolute, RoPE (rotates Q/K by position, encodes relative position — used by Llama/Gemma), and ALiBi (biases scores by distance).
distractors: Attention already encodes order via the causal mask, so positional encoding is only needed for encoder models like BERT.
Positional encoding stores each token's part-of-speech tag so the model knows its grammatical role.
It normalizes embedding magnitudes per position to keep the softmax temperature constant.
The main kinds are one-hot position vectors, TF-IDF weighting, and byte-pair position merges.
RoPE adds a learned bias to the feed-forward layer based on the absolute token index.
Positional encodings are timestamps of when each token was generated during decoding.
cards.quizAnswer: Order must be injected since attention is permutation-invariant; methods include sinusoidal, learned absolute, RoPE, and ALiBi.

cards.front: What is tokenization and why does it matter?
cards.back: Converting raw text into integer IDs using subword units (BPE or SentencePiece/Unigram): common words are one token, rare words split into pieces, and a byte fallback means nothing is out-of-vocabulary. Cost, context limits and chunking are all measured in tokens.
distractors: Splitting text strictly on whitespace and punctuation so each word becomes exactly one token.
Mapping every unique character to an ID, so the vocabulary is just the alphabet plus symbols.
Hashing each sentence into a fixed-length vector before it enters the embedding layer.
Using a fixed dictionary of words and replacing anything unknown with a single <UNK> token.
Compressing the text with gzip and feeding the resulting byte stream directly to the model.
Translating text into a controlled vocabulary of ~5,000 root words to reduce ambiguity.
cards.quizAnswer: Converting text to integer IDs via subword units (BPE/SentencePiece) so nothing is out-of-vocabulary; cost is counted in tokens.

cards.front: How does an LLM generate text? Explain decoding.
cards.back: Autoregressively: the model outputs a probability distribution over the next token, you pick one (greedy, beam search, or sampling with temperature / top-k / top-p), append it, and feed everything back in — repeating until an end-of-sequence token or length limit.
distractors: It generates the entire response in a single forward pass, emitting all tokens simultaneously.
It retrieves the closest matching sentence from its training corpus and lightly paraphrases it.
It fills masked positions bidirectionally like BERT, choosing the best token for every blank at once.
Temperature above 1 makes output more deterministic by sharpening the probability distribution.
Top-p sampling always draws from exactly the 50 most likely tokens regardless of confidence.
Beam search is the only valid decoder for chat models because greedy decoding cannot terminate.
cards.quizAnswer: Autoregressive next-token prediction, sampling each token (greedy, beam, temperature, top-k, top-p) and feeding it back until EOS.

cards.front: Difference between pretraining, fine-tuning, and in-context learning?
cards.back: Pretraining: self-supervised next-token training on trillions of tokens (broad knowledge, no task alignment). Fine-tuning: continued training on curated data to specialize (SFT, RLHF/DPO). In-context learning: no weight updates — examples/instructions live in the prompt; RAG is a form of it.
distractors: Pretraining and fine-tuning both update weights, while in-context learning permanently stores prompt examples in the model.
Fine-tuning trains from random initialization on your domain data; pretraining only adjusts the final layer.
In-context learning runs a few gradient steps on the prompt examples before answering, then discards them.
Pretraining is supervised on labeled (input, output) pairs; fine-tuning is the unsupervised next-token step.
RAG is a fine-tuning technique that bakes retrieved documents directly into the model's weights.
In-context learning needs at least 100 examples in the prompt or it is ignored entirely.
cards.quizAnswer: Pretraining learns broad knowledge; fine-tuning specializes via weight updates; in-context learning adapts from prompt examples, no updates.

cards.front: What are embeddings and how are they used?
cards.back: A dense vector representing the meaning of text, where semantic similarity corresponds to geometric closeness (cosine similarity). For RAG you embed documents once, store the vectors in a vector DB, embed the query at request time, and retrieve nearest neighbors.
distractors: A sparse one-hot vector with a 1 in the position of each word present in the text.
A compressed, JPEG-like encoding of token IDs used to save context-window space.
The raw softmax output distribution over the vocabulary for a given input.
A hash of the document used as a primary key in the vector database, with no semantic meaning.
A TF-IDF weighted bag-of-words vector, where similarity requires shared exact words.
The n×n attention matrix produced for a sentence, flattened into a single vector.
cards.quizAnswer: Dense vectors capturing meaning, where similarity is geometric closeness; used for semantic search and RAG retrieval.

cards.front: What is a context window, and what happens when you exceed it?
cards.back: The maximum number of tokens the model can attend to at once (prompt + output). It's bounded because attention is O(n²) in memory/compute. Exceed it and you must truncate, summarize, or retrieve (RAG). Note 'lost in the middle': models attend best to the start and end.
distractors: The maximum number of separate conversations a model can hold in memory simultaneously.
A sliding window of the last 512 tokens that the model is hard-coded to ignore beyond.
Exceeding it triggers an automatic fine-tune that extends the model's knowledge.
The number of GPUs required to serve the model; doubling context halves the GPU count.
Models attend most strongly to the exact middle of the context, so ordering rarely matters.
It is fixed by the tokenizer's vocabulary size rather than by attention cost.
cards.quizAnswer: The max tokens attended to at once; exceed it and you truncate, summarize, or retrieve (RAG). Attention is O(n²).

cards.back: Because an LLM is a next-token probability model, not a knowledge base — it generates the most plausible continuation, and plausible ≠ true, with no built-in sense of its knowledge boundary. You manage (not eliminate) it via RAG grounding, citations, an abstain path, low temperature, verification and evals.
distractors: Because the training data contained deliberate lies that the model memorized verbatim.
Because temperature is always set above 1, which forces random word choices.
Because the context window overflowed and corrupted the stored facts.
It is a bug in the attention mask that can be fully eliminated by retraining.
Because embeddings lose precision in float16, flipping true facts to false ones.
Because the model queries the internet at inference time and copies unreliable pages.
cards.quizAnswer: It's a next-token probability model, not a knowledge base, so it generates plausible-but-untrue text; manage with grounding and evals.

cards.front: What's the difference between RLHF, DPO, and instruction tuning?
cards.back: Instruction tuning (SFT): supervised on (instruction, good response) pairs — teaches format/task. RLHF: train a reward model on human preference rankings, then use RL (PPO) toward high reward. DPO: optimizes directly on preference pairs with a classification-style loss — no separate reward model and no RL loop.
distractors: RLHF needs no human input; its reward model is trained on automatically generated labels.
DPO uses reinforcement learning with a reward model but skips the supervised step.
Instruction tuning aligns tone and harmlessness, while RLHF only teaches output formatting.
DPO requires three models in the loop, making it more complex than RLHF.
RLHF and DPO update no weights; they only change the system prompt.
Instruction tuning is unsupervised next-token prediction on raw web text.
cards.quizAnswer: SFT teaches format/task from pairs; RLHF uses a reward model plus RL; DPO optimizes preferences directly, no reward model.

cards.front: What is the KV cache and why does it matter for serving?
cards.back: During generation each new token attends to all previous tokens' Keys and Values; the KV cache stores past K/V so each step only computes them for the new token, cutting per-step cost from O(n²) toward O(n). It's the dominant inference memory consumer and grows with sequence length and batch size — why vLLM/PagedAttention exist.
distractors: A cache of recent user prompts and responses kept to answer repeat questions instantly.
It stores the model's weights in faster memory so the GPU skips reloading them each step.
It caches the final softmax distribution so identical prompts produce identical outputs.
A disk-based cache of embeddings for the retrieval layer, unrelated to generation.
It precomputes Keys and Values for all possible future tokens to speed up sampling.
The KV cache shrinks as the sequence grows, which is why long contexts are cheap.
cards.quizAnswer: Caches past tokens' Keys/Values so each step computes only the new token; the main inference memory cost, growing with length.

cards.front: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) — what is it, in one breath?
cards.back: Instead of one big feed-forward net per layer, an MoE layer has many expert FFNs plus a lightweight router that sends each token to only a couple of them — a huge total parameter count but only a small fraction active per token, giving big-model capacity at smaller-model inference cost (e.g. Mixtral).
distractors: An ensemble of several complete models that all run on every input and vote on the output.
A technique that trains a separate model per language and picks one by detected language.
A router that selects which GPU to use, with every expert still seeing every token.
Replacing attention with many small expert attention heads to cut the O(n²) cost.
A cascade where a small model answers easy queries and escalates hard ones to a large model.
Splitting the vocabulary across experts so each handles a subset of possible tokens.
cards.quizAnswer: Many expert feed-forward nets with a router sending each token to a few; huge capacity, small fraction active per token.
